         f, ax = plt.subplots()

problem_statement.Title: pairplot raises TypeError when hue_order omits some hue values (isnan on non-numeric keys)
problem_statement.Description: Passing a hue_order that does not include all values present in the hue column (for example, plotting a subset of categorical levels) can raise a TypeError: "ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types...". The exception occurs when the hue-to-color mapping falls through to code that calls numpy.isnan on a non-numeric (string/object) key.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) seaborn/_oldcore.py :: HueMapping._lookup_single assumes keys can be tested with numpy's isnan and therefore calls np.isnan(key) in the KeyError / TypeError fallback. np.isnan raises a TypeError for string/object keys, producing the observed "ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types" error.

2) In the same method (seaborn/_oldcore.py :: HueMapping._lookup_single), the code attempts a numeric fallback by calling self.norm(key) unconditionally when a key is not found in the categorical lookup_table. For categorical mappings self.norm is None (set in the constructor), so calling self.norm(key) raises TypeError; that TypeError is then routed to the np.isnan(key) branch, which in turn raises the ufunc TypeError for non-numeric keys. The unconditional call to self.norm(key) is thus part of the root cause.

3) HueMapping._lookup_single uses the hardcoded transparent RGBA tuple (0, 0, 0, 0) to represent missing/NA values in the np.isnan-based branch. The presence of this transparent-return behavior is consistent with prior behavior (omitted hue levels resulting in effectively not-drawn points), but the NA test used (np.isnan) is not dtype-safe for arbitrary hue key types. The NA test should be dtype-aware (e.g., pandas.isna) to avoid the numpy ufunc TypeError. All three findings are independent points of diagnosis located at: seaborn/_oldcore.py :: HueMapping._lookup_single.
StepsToReproduce: Observe that in seaborn 0.11.1 this raises: TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types ...
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When hue_order is a subset of the unique values in the hue column, seaborn should not raise a TypeError. Points whose hue value is not in hue_order should be treated as missing/omitted (effectively not drawn) and the plot and legend should reflect only the requested hue levels.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling pairplot (or other plotting routines that rely on hue mapping) with hue_order omitting some hue values raises a TypeError: "ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types...". The traceback originates from seaborn/_oldcore.py :: HueMapping._lookup_single calling np.isnan on a non-numeric (string/object) key.

hints_text:
The following workarounds seem to work:
```
g.map(sns.scatterplot, hue=iris["species"], hue_order=iris["species"].unique())
```
or
```
g.map(lambda x, y, **kwargs: sns.scatterplot(x=x, y=y, hue=iris["species"]))
```
> ```
> g.map(sns.scatterplot, hue=iris["species"], hue_order=iris["species"].unique())
> ```

The workaround fixes the problem for me.
Thank you very much!

@mwaskom Should I close the Issue or leave it open until the bug is fixed?
That's a good workaround, but it's still a bug. The problem is that `PairGrid` now lets `hue` at the grid-level delegate to the axes-level functions if they have `hue` in their signature. But it's not properly handling the case where `hue` is *not* set for the grid, but *is* specified for one mapped function. @jhncls's workaround suggests the fix.

An easier workaround would have been to set `PairGrid(..., hue="species")` and then pass `.map(..., hue=None)` where you don't want to separate by species. But `regplot` is the one axis-level function that does not yet handle hue-mapping internally, so it doesn't work for this specific case. It would have if you wanted a single bivariate density over hue-mapped scatterplot points (i.e. [this example](http://seaborn.pydata.org/introduction.html#classes-and-functions-for-making-complex-graphics) or something similar.

patch:
diff --git a/seaborn/_stats/regression.py b/seaborn/_stats/regression.py
--- a/seaborn/_stats/regression.py
+++ b/seaborn/_stats/regression.py
@@ -38,7 +38,10 @@ def _fit_predict(self, data):
 
     def __call__(self, data, groupby, orient, scales):
 
-        return groupby.apply(data, self._fit_predict)
+        return (
+            groupby
+            .apply(data.dropna(subset=["x", "y"]), self._fit_predict)
+        )
 
 
 @dataclass

+        res2 = PolyFit()(df[["x", "y"]].dropna(), groupby, "x", {})
+        assert_frame_equal(res1, res2)

problem_statement.Title: PolyFit raises LinAlgError when x or y contain missing values
problem_statement.Description: Calling PolyFit on data where x or y contain None/NaN causes numpy.linalg.LinAlgError (SVD did not converge) during np.polyfit inside seaborn._stats.regression.PolyFit._fit_predict.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) seaborn/_stats/regression.py :: PolyFit._fit_predict — The method extracts x = data["x"] and y = data["y"] and calls numpy.polyfit(x, y, self.order) directly on these Series (line 30). It does not remove rows where x or y are missing before calling np.polyfit; passing Series containing NaN/None into numpy.polyfit (and the underlying linear-algebra routines) can raise LinAlgError ("SVD did not converge").

2) seaborn/_stats/regression.py :: PolyFit._fit_predict — The existing insufficient-data guard inspects x.nunique() relative to self.order (line 26) but does so on the unfiltered Series. The correct data relationship for deciding whether a fit is possible is based on pairs where both x and y are present (valid = x.notna() & y.notna()); the sufficiency test must be applied to x[valid] (e.g., check x[valid].nunique() <= self.order to decide not to fit).

3) seaborn/_stats/regression.py :: PolyFit._fit_predict — When the method decides not to fit it sets xx = yy = [] (plain Python lists; line 28) and returns pd.DataFrame(dict(x=xx, y=yy)). Downstream code (plot compilation and scale setup) expects numeric coordinate columns or empty numeric Series/arrays when no fit is produced; returning empty Python lists can lead to ambiguous/ non-numeric dtypes in the resulting DataFrame and complicate scale inference and downstream consumers.

4) seaborn/_stats/regression.py :: PolyFit class attributes — PolyFit.order (default=2, line 19) determines the polynomial degree and therefore encodes the data requirement of at least order+1 distinct x values to fit; PolyFit.gridsize (default=100, line 20) controls the number of sample points on the predicted curve. Data-sufficiency checks must operate with these parameters in mind and must be applied to the filtered, non-missing pairs referenced above.
StepsToReproduce: In a Python REPL or script with seaborn imported, run:
Attempt to render or display the Plot object (e.g. in an interactive notebook) and observe the traceback.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Plotting with PolyFit should not raise an exception when the input contains missing values. The polynomial should be fitted using only rows where both x and y are present; if there is insufficient valid data (fewer than order+1 distinct x values) the plot should gracefully show no fit for that layer (or otherwise omit the fitted curve) without raising an internal linear-algebra error.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling PolyFit on data containing None/NaN leads to an exception during plotting: LinAlgError: SVD did not converge in Linear Least Squares (raised by numpy.linalg when np.polyfit receives inputs containing NaN or degenerate data). The stack trace points to seaborn/_stats/regression.py PolyFit._fit_predict -> np.polyfit.

diff --git a/seaborn/_core/scales.py b/seaborn/_core/scales.py
--- a/seaborn/_core/scales.py
+++ b/seaborn/_core/scales.py
@@ -346,7 +346,7 @@ def _setup(
                 vmin, vmax = data.min(), data.max()
             else:
                 vmin, vmax = new.norm
-            vmin, vmax = axis.convert_units((vmin, vmax))
+            vmin, vmax = map(float, axis.convert_units((vmin, vmax)))
             a = forward(vmin)
             b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin)

problem_statement.Title: Color mapping fails for boolean data due to incorrect default scale selection
problem_statement.Description: Providing boolean values for the color aesthetic (e.g. color=[True, False]) raises a TypeError during scale setup. The error occurs when the plotting machinery selects a Continuous scale for boolean data and then performs numeric operations on boolean arrays.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Property.default_scale calls variable_type(data, boolean_type="numeric") and therefore treats boolean-only vectors as numeric by default. Location: seaborn/_core/properties.py, Property.default_scale (var_type = variable_type(data, boolean_type="numeric")). The hardcoded argument is the string "numeric".

2) The Color class does not override default_scale and so inherits Property.default_scale; but Color.infer_scale calls variable_type(data, boolean_type="categorical") (i.e., infer_scale treats booleans as categorical). This mismatch means an un-specified color scale can be chosen based on the parent's boolean handling rather than Color's own handling. Location: seaborn/_core/properties.py, class Color (no default_scale override) and Color.infer_scale (uses boolean_type="categorical").

3) Plotter uses prop.default_scale when no explicit scale is provided, then calls scale._setup(data, prop). Because prop.default_scale can return a Continuous scale for boolean color data, Plotter will invoke ContinuousBase._setup on boolean values. Location: seaborn/_core/plot.py, Plotter._get_scale (calls prop.default_scale) and Plotter._setup_scales (calls scale._setup(...)).

4) ContinuousBase._setup assumes numeric endpoints and computes the normalization width using the expression b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin). If forward(vmin)/forward(vmax) are boolean arrays, subtraction is unsupported and raises the observed TypeError. Location: seaborn/_core/scales.py, ContinuousBase._setup (a = forward(vmin); b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin)).

Related observation: some other semantic properties explicitly default to a Nominal scale for boolean-like inputs (for example, ObjectProperty.default_scale returns Nominal() and Fill.default_scale returns Nominal()), indicating inconsistency in boolean handling across properties. Location: seaborn/_core/properties.py, ObjectProperty.default_scale, Fill.default_scale.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following in a Python session with seaborn codebase checked out and importable:
Observe the traceback raised during plotting/scale setup.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Providing boolean values for the color aesthetic should not raise an error. Boolean values should be treated as discrete categories for color mapping so that the plot renders and a distinct color is assigned for True and False.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A TypeError is raised during scale setup: "numpy boolean subtract, the `-` operator, is not supported...". The traceback shows ContinuousBase._setup computing b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin) on boolean values, causing the failure.

hints_text: Would this simply mean refactoring the code to use `^` or `xor` functions instead?

                 for ax in diag_axes[1:]:
                     share_axis(diag_axes[0], ax, "y")
 
-            self.diag_vars = np.array(diag_vars, np.object_)
-            self.diag_axes = np.array(diag_axes, np.object_)
+            self.diag_vars = diag_vars

         if "hue" not in signature(func).parameters:
             return self._map_diag_iter_hue(func, **kwargs)

problem_statement.Title: pairplot / PairGrid raises KeyError when DataFrame has MultiIndex columns (tuple labels)
problem_statement.Description: Calling seaborn.pairplot on a DataFrame whose columns are a pandas.MultiIndex (so each column label is a tuple) raises KeyError during PairGrid plotting. The failure occurs when PairGrid (and related faceting code) index the DataFrame using bracket-access with a variable-derived key that is a tuple; pandas can interpret the tuple as an iterable of labels and attempt to look up each element, leading to KeyError (e.g. "['1'] not in index").
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Seaborn code assumes DataFrame.__getitem__ with a tuple key will be treated as a single label if that tuple matches a MultiIndex column. This assumption appears in multiple locations and is encoded by direct bracket-indexing expressions like data[var], self.data[var], data[hue], etc. Primary affected locations (file / class / method):
   1. seaborn/axisgrid.py : PairGrid.map_diag (map_diag uses vector = self.data[var]) — map_diag passes a tuple column label as key to DataFrame.__getitem__.
   2. seaborn/axisgrid.py : PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue (uses hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(...)).
   3. seaborn/axisgrid.py : PairGrid._plot_bivariate and PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue (use data = self.data[axes_vars] and x = data[x_var], y = data[y_var]). These perform bracket-access where x_var/y_var may be tuple labels.
   4. seaborn/axisgrid.py : PairGrid._find_numeric_cols (iterates columns with for col in data: then calls variable_type(data[col]) — bracket-access with col may be a tuple label).
   5. seaborn/axisgrid.py : FacetGrid.facet_data (uses data[self._row_var], data[self._col_var], data[self._hue_var] to build masks).
   6. seaborn/axisgrid.py : Grid._get_palette (uses categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order)).
   7. seaborn/_oldcore.py : VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform (uses plot_data[key] = data[val] when val in data; val may be a tuple label).

   Why this is wrong: pandas may interpret a tuple passed to DataFrame.__getitem__ as an iterable of column keys rather than a single MultiIndex label (in some pandas versions/contexts). For example, data[('A','1')] may be treated as data[['A','1']] internally, causing pandas to look for '1' as a top-level column label and raising KeyError if not found. The correct relationship is: when selecting a column by label where columns are a MultiIndex, selection must treat the full tuple as the label; if bracket-access raises KeyError/TypeError indicating tuple expansion, code should fall back to label-based indexing (data.loc[:, key]) or otherwise perform a selection that respects tuple labels.

   Preserved formula/relationship: when retrieving a single column identified by key (which may be a tuple), the code must obtain a single Series corresponding to that exact label. In case bracket access fails due to tuple being interpreted as iterable, the code should use data.loc[:, key] as a fallback to select by label rather than by iterating key's elements.
